questions.question: Why are satellite constellations needed for global QKD?

options.text: To provide continuous coverage and reduce revisit time
Single satellites cannot reach high altitudes

questions.explanation: A single LEO satellite can only serve a given ground station for ~10 minutes per pass, with hours between passes. Constellations with multiple satellites in different orbital planes provide continuous or near-continuous global coverage.

questions.question: What is an inter-satellite link (ISL) used for in quantum networks?

options.text: To connect satellites for coordination and quantum relay

questions.explanation: Inter-satellite links connect satellites for classical coordination (timing, routing) and potentially quantum relay (entanglement swapping in space). This enables a satellite-based backbone for the quantum network.

questions.question: How many satellites might a global QKD constellation require?
questions.explanation: Practical global QKD constellations are estimated to need 12-50 satellites depending on coverage requirements. This is much smaller than communications constellations like Starlink because QKD needs clear sky and can tolerate some gaps.

questions.question: What is the advantage of sun-synchronous orbits for QKD?

options.text: Consistent sun angle provides predictable lighting conditions

questions.explanation: Sun-synchronous orbits maintain a constant angle to the sun, providing predictable and consistent lighting conditions. Dawn-dusk orbits minimize sunlight directly on the optical path, which helps with background noise.

questions.question: In a hybrid network, what technology is best for metropolitan distances (<100 km)?
questions.explanation: For metropolitan distances under 100 km, fiber QKD is optimal. It provides 24/7 availability, high key rates (Mbps possible), and doesn't depend on weather. Satellites are overkill for short distances.

questions.question: Why is there no atmospheric loss for inter-satellite links?

options.text: The link travels through vacuum in space

questions.explanation: Inter-satellite links travel entirely through the vacuum of space, so there is no atmospheric absorption or scattering. The only loss is geometric (beam spreading), which is manageable with proper optics.

questions.question: A global quantum network will use only satellites without any fiber infrastructure.
questions.explanation: False. Hybrid networks combining satellite and fiber are optimal. Fiber handles local/metropolitan distances (24/7, high rate), while satellites handle intercontinental distances where fiber isn't practical.
